{{Short description|Psychological measurement}}

In [[social psychology]], the '''liking gap''' is the disparity between how much a person believes that another person likes them and that other person's actual opinion.<ref name="Boothby et al. 2018" /> Studies have found that most people underestimate how much other people like them and enjoy their company.<ref name="Boothby et al. 2018" /><ref>{{cite news |title=Bridging the 'liking-gap,' researchers discuss awkwardness of conversations |url=https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2019/02/190208115331.htm |work=Science Daily}}</ref><ref name="Wolf et al. 2021">{{Cite journal |last1=Wolf |first1=Wouter |last2=Nafe |first2=Amanda |last3=Tomasello |first3=Michael |date=2021-04-29 |title=The Development of the Liking Gap: Children Older Than 5 Years Think That Partners Evaluate Them Less Positively Than They Evaluate Their Partners |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1177/0956797620980754 |journal=Psychological Science |volume=32 |issue=5 |pages=789–798 |doi=10.1177/0956797620980754 |pmid=33914647 |s2cid=233462197 |issn=0956-7976|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==Research==
The 2018 ''[[Psychological Science]]'' study which coined the term "liking gap" explored people's interactions in various scenarios: strangers meeting for the first time in a laboratory setting, members of the general public getting to know each other during a personal development workshop, and first-year college students living with a dormmate for one academic year.<ref name="Boothby et al. 2018" /> In all three scenarios, participants consistently self-assessed as less liked by the other person than they actually were.<ref name="Boothby et al. 2018">{{Cite journal |last1=Boothby |first1=Erica J. |last2=Cooney |first2=Gus |last3=Sandstrom |first3=Gillian M. |last4=Clark |first4=Margaret S. |date=2018-11-01 |title=The Liking Gap in Conversations: Do People Like Us More Than We Think? |url=http://repository.essex.ac.uk/23025/1/The%20Liking%20Gap%20in%20Conversations.pdf |journal=[[Psychological Science]] |language=en |volume=29 |issue=11 |pages=1742–1756 |doi=10.1177/0956797618783714 |issn=0956-7976 |pmid=30183512 |s2cid=52165115}}</ref> The gap was shown to be present in short, medium, and long conversations among strangers.<ref name="Boothby et al. 2018" /> In the laboratory setting, participants reporting both high and average shyness liked their discussion partner significantly more than they assessed their partner to like them.<ref name="Boothby et al. 2018" /> In students, the liking gap persisted nearly the entire duration of the study before suddenly closing at the end – possibly indicating that dormmates had directly discussed interpersonal compatibility to decide whether they should share a dorm the next year.<ref name="Boothby et al. 2018" /> The study found that participants gave each other [[Signalling theory|social signals]] indicating liking but that these signals were neglected.<ref name="Boothby et al. 2018" />

There is evidence that suggests the liking gap begins to develop from the age of 5, as this is around the time when children begin to become more aware of and concerned with the ways that they are evaluated by others.<ref name="Wolf et al. 2021" /> 

Research suggests that people usually have favorable views about themselves and others.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Alicke|first=Mark|date=1985|title=Global self-evaluation as determined by the desirability and controllability of trait adjectives|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=49|issue=6|pages=1621–1630|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.49.6.1621}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kruger|first1=Justin|last2=David|first2=Dunning|date=1999|title=Unskilled and unaware of it: how difficulties in recognizing one's own incompetence lead to inflated self-assessments.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=77|issue=6|pages=1121–1134|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.77.6.1121|pmid=10626367}}</ref> However, there is evidence that people tend to exhibit [[self-criticism]] when thinking about their own interactions with others.<ref name="Boothby et al. 2018" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Deri|first1=Sebastian|last2=Davidai|first2=Shai|last3=Gilovich|first3=Thomas|date=2017|title=Home alone: Why people believe others' social lives are richer than their own.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=113|issue=6|pages=858–877|doi=10.1037/pspa0000105|pmid=29189037|s2cid=25964432}}</ref>
